quoteFirst: “The actions,” Captain Berry relates, “commenced at sunset, which was at
quoteLast: , p.m., with an ardour and vigour which it is impossible to describe. At about seven o’clock total darkness had come on, but the whole hemisphere was, with intervals, illuminated by the fire of the hostile Fleets. Our Ships, when darkness came on, had all hoisted their distinguishing lights, by a signal from the Admiral. The Van ship of the Enemy, _Le Guerrier_, was dismasted in less than twelve minutes, and, in ten minutes after, the second ship, _Le Conquérant_, and the third, _Le Spartiate_, very nearly at the same moment were almost dismasted. _L’Aquilon_ and _Le Peuple Souverain_, the fourth and fifth Ships of the Enemy’s line, were taken possession of by the British at half-past eight in the evening. Captain Berry, at that hour, sent Lieutenant Galwey, of the _Vanguard_, with a
title: The Boys' Nelson
author: Wheeler, Harold, 1877-

quoteLast: , mamma. I wish you would put your watch on of a day; four times you have sent me to look at that clock since dinner.”

quoteLast: , so much of the Sun's Body will be darkened, as the 12th represents: and at 36 past 6, will be the greatest darkness, when only a small thread of Light will be seen at _London_, on the upper part of the _Sun_ as the 13th Scheme informs; but to all the Southern parts of the Kingdom, it will be totally darkened.
